text.title: STAR Interview Method Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules grounding the STAR Interview Method methodology. Each rule pairs a binary check + sourced rationale. Hard rules — user-supplied input cannot override them.

rule.statement: For every story the candidate tells, probe Situation/Task/Action/Result completeness explicitly; reject pronoun ambiguity ('we did X' → 'what did YOU do?').
rule.rationale: STAR's diagnostic power depends on completeness; partial stories (S + R only) leave action invisible and surface team narratives instead of individual evidence.

rule.statement: Notes are evidence-anchored: each scorecard line cites a specific quote, action, or number from the story — not the interviewer's interpretation.
rule.rationale: Anchored notes make debrief defensible; interpretive notes ('seemed driven') collapse on push-back.

rule.statement: Submit the scorecard within 24h of the round; later submission contaminates with other candidates and post-hoc rationalization.
rule.rationale: Memory decay is fastest in the first 24h; latency in submission has measurable effect on inter-rater disagreement.

rule.statement: Each behavioral round covers ≥2 competencies in 45-60 minutes; one-competency rounds waste candidate and interviewer time and force per-round overhead.
rule.rationale: Cost-per-signal is what the interview process optimizes; coverage breadth per round dominates other tactical choices.

rule.statement: Hire/no-hire recommendation MUST be present on the scorecard with at least two cited pieces of evidence; 'lean hire' without evidence is rejected.
rule.rationale: Recommendations without evidence push the debrief into 'whose gut do we trust' territory; cited evidence forces calibration.
